era_context: Akkadian Empire under Naram-Sin in its final decades; Old Kingdom Egypt (6th Dynasty) in terminal decline; Harappan civilization in mature phase; Xia Dynasty traditionally dated to this era (unconfirmed). This year falls within the broader period of the 4.2 kya climate event, which is beginning to impact agricultural societies across Eurasia and Africa.

events.title: Naram-Sin's reign continues; Akkadian military campaigns and consolidation
events.description: Naram-Sin (r. c. 2254–2218 BCE) continues his military campaigns and consolidation of Akkadian power. He maintains control over Mesopotamian city-states and extends Akkadian influence into the Levant and possibly Anatolia. His deification and the associated ideological shift represent a significant development in Mesopotamian kingship. However, signs of strain are beginning to appear: military campaigns become more frequent and costly, and evidence suggests increasing pressure from external groups (Gutians, Amorites) on the empire's periphery.

sources.name: Akkadian royal inscriptions and administrative texts

events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's reign is well-attested, but the precise chronology and dating of individual campaigns remain uncertain. The year -2201 is assigned by convention within his reign.
cross_references: Part of broader Akkadian imperial period

events.title: Egyptian 6th Dynasty in decline; provincial autonomy increasing
events.description: The 6th Dynasty of Egypt continues its decline. Pepi II, the traditional last pharaoh of the 6th Dynasty (r. c. 2278–2184 BCE), is in his reign. Central authority weakens further as nomarchs accumulate power and resources. Pyramid construction has ceased. The Nile's inundation remains erratic, exacerbating economic stress. Regional fragmentation accelerates.

sources.name: Egyptian king lists and tomb inscriptions
Paleoclimatic records indicating reduced Nile inundation

events.certainty_note: The 6th Dynasty's decline is well-documented, but precise dating within the dynasty remains uncertain. The year -2201 is assigned as an approximate marker.

events.title: Harappan civilization continues in mature phase; urban centers thriving
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization remains in its mature phase. Urban centers continue to flourish. Trade networks remain active, connecting the Indus Valley to Mesopotamia and the Persian Gulf. Standardized weights and measures indicate sophisticated economic administration. No major disruptions are evident at this time, though some scholars suggest that environmental stress (possibly linked to the 4.2 kya event) may be beginning to affect agricultural productivity in some regions.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Indus Valley sites

events.certainty_note: The Harappan civilization's continued flourishing is well-attested archaeologically. However, the absence of written records limits understanding of specific events or developments.
cross_references: Part of broader Harappan mature phase

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the precise dating of Naram-Sin's reign and the Egyptian 6th Dynasty remains subject to scholarly debate, with variations of ±10–20 years depending on chronological system used.
historiographic_note: The year -2201 is poorly differentiated from -2200 in the historical record. Most significant events of this period are dated to broader ranges (e.g., 'c. 2200 BCE' or 'late 3rd millennium BCE') rather than to specific years. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward Mesopotamia and Egypt; other regions are underrepresented. The 4.2 kya climate event is confirmed in paleoclimatic records but its impact on specific societies in this particular year is not documented.

graph_edges.note: Naram-Sin's reign continues toward its culmination and the Akkadian collapse
